one_line_takeaway: Combining multiple independently fingerprinted LLMs into an ensemble via simple token-level intersection-filtering (TFA) or perplexity-based majority voting (SVA) can drive backdoor-fingerprint verification success down to 0% (up to 100% ASR) essentially for free, while matching or beating the best individual model's downstream accuracy, exposing an unaddressed weakness in current LLM IP-protection schemes.
key_finding: Effectiveness (Table 2, 12 fingerprinted ensembles): TFA achieves 100% ASR across all three fingerprinting methods (IF, C&H, ImF). SVA achieves 98% average ASR on IF and 100% on C&H, but drops to a 78% average (with per-model values as low as 50%) on ImF because ImF's fingerprint responses are semantically closer to normal responses and thus harder to separate by PPL. Harmlessness (Section 5.3, Figure 5): both attacks preserve or improve downstream accuracy versus baseline; TFA consistently matches or exceeds the best individual model across all combinations, while SVA achieves this only depending on the auxiliary-model selection strategy, with negligible degradation observed for Qwen2.5-7B-It under SVA. Baseline comparison (Table 3): incremental fine-tuning achieves 0% ASR (fails entirely); GRI achieves 100% ASR only on IF (keyword-detectable trigger) but 0% on C&H/ImF; MEraser reaches 90-100% ASR but needs per-fingerprint-method hyperparameter retuning; Merge attack is highly ratio-sensitive (e.g., 100% ASR only at a specific 5:5 ratio for Qwen2.5-7B/ImF, 0% at other ratios); UniTE swings from 0% to 100% ASR depending on model/fingerprint pairing. By contrast SVA and TFA are reported as consistently achieving 90-100% ASR with no per-method parameter tuning required. Ablations: increasing auxiliary models from 2 to 4 does not meaningfully raise ASR but does raise downstream accuracy and cost (Figure 6); TFA remains robust when auxiliary models change (Figure 7) and when all ensemble members share the same fingerprinting method (Figure 8), whereas SVA's ASR degrades notably in the same-method setting; increasing TFA's top-K from 20 to 30 causes a modest ASR drop (100%->90%) for one ImF ensemble, but the authors report top-K in the 10-30 range as broadly stable (Section 6.4). An appendix extension (Table 8) against a fourth fingerprinting method, CTCC (cross-turn contextual correlation, Xu et al. 2025), shows SVA/TFA still achieve 100% ASR in most configurations versus weaker baseline performance.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is two novel attacks against backdoor-based LLM fingerprinting when the fingerprinted models are deployed inside an LLM ensemble: (1) Token Filter Attack (TFA), a during-inference, token-level attack that at each decoding step intersects the top-K token sets (with probabilities) produced by every model in the ensemble, unions the pairwise intersections into a unified vocabulary V_U, recomputes a per-model probability distribution restricted to V_U (dropping tokens not in V_U), and averages these distributions across models to pick the next token; and (2) Sentence Verification Attack (SVA), an after-inference, sentence-level attack that collects each model's candidate response to a query, has every other model score each candidate's perplexity (PPL), keeps the lowest-PPL candidate per model, tallies selection frequency across models (majority voting), and outputs the most-selected response, exploiting the empirical observation that fingerprint-triggered responses have markedly higher PPL than normal responses. The authors frame this as the first work to study fingerprinting robustness specifically in the LLM-ensemble deployment setting (Before/During/After-inference ensembling), as opposed to prior single-model attacks.
threat_model: Stated explicitly in the paper's Section 3 ("Threat Model"): Defenders are model owners who embed backdoor-based fingerprints (secret trigger-response pairs (x,y)) into their LLMs via supervised fine-tuning (full-parameter or LoRA) and can only query suspect models via API. Attackers have unauthorized possession of the fingerprinted models and combine them into an ensemble; their two goals are (a) achieve 100% fingerprint-verification failure across all individual models in the ensemble, and (b) preserve ensemble utility, matching or exceeding the performance of the best individual model on downstream tasks. Attackers operate under two cognitive constraints: complete lack of knowledge of the trigger strategy/fingerprint content, and awareness that every model in the ensemble may carry a fingerprint. This is a black-box, parameter-free (non-parameter-modification) attack setting distinct from prior fine-tuning/merging-based fingerprint-erasure attacks.
limitations: Stated in the paper's own Limitation section: (1) SVA's attack success rate decreases when all models in the ensemble use the same fingerprinting technique (only differing in trigger content) rather than diverse techniques; (2) ensemble downstream performance does not exceed the best individual model when there is a large performance gap between the primary and auxiliary models. The paper also notes SVA underperforms specifically on ImF (as low as 50% ASR for some model/auxiliary combinations) because ImF's steganographically-hidden fingerprint responses have PPL close to normal responses, making PPL-based filtering less reliable. The evaluated fingerprinting methods (IF, C&H, ImF, CTCC) are all backdoor/SFT-based; the paper does not evaluate attacks against inherent (non-backdoor) fingerprinting. All experiments use 7-8B (and smaller auxiliary) open-weight models; no results are reported for closed/very large frontier models.

datasets_used: Alpaca-GPT4-52k (used as fine-tuning data for the incremental fine-tuning baseline attack, not by the paper's own TFA/SVA methods)
quantitative_results: TFA: 100% ASR across all three fingerprinting methods (IF, C&H, ImF) on the 12-ensemble main evaluation (Table 2)
SVA: 98% avg ASR on IF, 100% avg ASR on C&H, 78% avg ASR on ImF (Table 2), with per-model lows of 50%
Incremental fine-tuning baseline: 0% ASR (all fingerprinting methods)
GRI baseline: 100% ASR on IF, 0% ASR on C&H and ImF
MEraser baseline: 90-100% ASR across methods, but requires per-method hyperparameter tuning
Merge attack baseline: up to 100% ASR only at specific merge ratios (e.g., 5:5 for Qwen2.5-7B/ImF), 0% at other ratios (4:6, 6:4)
UniTE baseline: 0-100% ASR, highly variable by model/fingerprint combination
TFA top-K ablation: ASR drops from 100% to 90% when top-K increases from 20 to 30 for one ImF-Qwen2.5-7B ensemble; stable in the 10-30 range overall
CTCC extension (Table 8, appendix): SVA/TFA achieve up to 100% ASR versus GRI 0%, MEraser up to 100%, Merge attack up to 40%, UniTE up to 100% but inconsistent
baselines_compared: GRI (Wu et al. 2025, chain-of-thought prompting attack)
MEraser (Zhang et al. 2025, two-phase fine-tuning erasure)
Merge attack / Task Arithmetic (Yamabe et al. 2024, MergePrint)
UniTE (Yao et al. 2024, top-K union ensembling without pairwise intersection)
